one_line_takeaway: IHO trains a black-box, DPO-optimized masked-diffusion attacker model that outperforms GCG+, AmpleGCG+, PAIR(+), BON, JR1+, and INPAINTING baselines on defended LLM targets (e.g., 0.82 vs <=0.63 EVUS on Circuit Breaker, 0.77 vs <=0.55 on Circuit Breaker+detector) while transferring zero-shot across both held-out behaviors and unseen target models, and the paper additionally proposes EVUS as a threshold- and sample-efficiency-aware replacement for plain jailbreak Attack Success Rate.
key_finding: On the 60 training behaviors, IHO matches or beats the strongest baseline (JR1+) on undefended models and opens a large gap on defended ones, measured in EVUS under StrongReject: against Circuit Breaker, IHO reaches 0.82 EVUS vs. no baseline exceeding 0.63; adding the PolyGuard detector (CB+D) drops every baseline below 0.55 while IHO retains 0.77; on LAT, 0.78 (IHO) vs. 0.63 (next best); on CAT, 0.79 (IHO) vs. 0.70 (next best). On the 40 held-out (unseen during training) behaviors with the same trained checkpoints, IHO transfers with comparable or slightly better scores (e.g., 0.82 on CB and 0.79 on CB+D), demonstrating cross-behavior generalization without per-behavior retraining. In cross-model transfer (Figure 3), policies trained on one source model and evaluated on a different, unseen target model still match or exceed all baselines, with the largest margin on the most robust targets (baselines drop to 0.44-0.64 EVUS while transferred IHO policies mostly exceed 0.70); transfer is strongest when source and target have similar robustness levels, and degrades bidirectionally under large robustness mismatch. On efficiency, IHO's amortized training cost on Qwen-2.5-7B is broken into DPO training (7.49e17 FLOPs/behavior), cycle sampling (2.21e17), and DPO sampling (3.78e16) per training behavior; switching from the INPAINTING baseline's 64-token/75-step configuration to IHO's 32-token/32-step configuration is reported as an approximately 76% reduction in per-sample inference FLOPs. IHO also reaches near-ceiling cumulative EASR(*, 0.8) within about 100 sampled generations, ahead of JR1+ (closest competitor) and well ahead of GCG+/PAIR+, which plateau lower. A perplexity analysis (Figure 5b-c) shows that over DPO cycles the attacker's prompts and the resulting target outputs shift from a wide range including a long tail of high-perplexity (incoherent) prompts/outputs toward fluent, low-perplexity prompts that elicit high-harm responses, i.e., the model is not merely degrading target coherence to force refusal bypass.
core_contribution: The paper introduces Indirect Harm Optimization (IHO), a black-box jailbreak attack that trains a masked diffusion language model (LLaDA-8B-Base) as a parameterized 'attacker policy' via iterative Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) against an LLM harmfulness judge (StrongReject). The attacker is conditioned directly on a target harmful behavior description and generates adversarial prompts through bidirectional infilling, decoupling optimization from the target model (no gradients through the target needed) so it works against arbitrary black-box defense pipelines, including non-differentiable ones like a detector-wrapped model. The same trained policy can be used as (a) an adaptive attack fine-tuned per target model/behavior set, or (b) an amortized policy that transfers zero-shot to held-out behaviors and to unseen target models. The paper also proposes a new evaluation metric, Expected Volume Under the Surface (EVUS), which integrates expected attack success over both judge thresholds and sample budgets (via an exact hypergeometric 'analytical resampling' estimator) to fix four identified pathologies of plain Attack Success Rate (ASR): threshold sensitivity, severity blindness, sample-efficiency blindness, and false-positive collapse at large n.
threat_model: Black-box access only: the adversary observes the target model's decoded text output y given a prompt p, with no gradients, logits, or weights (K desideratum). The target can be a single undefended instruction-tuned LLM or a layered defense pipeline (safety-trained/adversarially-trained model plus an external input/output detector) that need not be differentiable. The attacker's own training-time optimization uses only sampled completions scored by an external judge model (StrongReject, with HarmBench-Llama-2-13B as a secondary judge in the appendix), never gradients from the target. Behaviors are drawn from JailbreakBench (100 malicious behaviors, split 60 train / 40 held-out, stratified by category).
limitations: The paper's own stated limitations: (1) cross-model transfer degrades when source and target models differ substantially in robustness, so a single universal transfer policy does not fully replace per-target adaptive training in the most asymmetric source/target pairs; (2) although Appendix F.2 reports no evidence of judge hacking in their experiments, the optimization signal is fundamentally dependent on the judge model's quality, and judge biases could shape what the attacker learns; (3) authors note as future work a controlled comparison between DPO and on-policy reinforcement learning under matched compute/query budgets (not performed in this paper), and testing sensitivity to the preference-loss choice (IPO, KTO, SimPO) versus plain DPO. Additionally, the EVUS metric and 76% FLOP-reduction claim both rest on the authors' own analytical/ablation framework rather than independent replication, and the evaluation is restricted to JailbreakBench's 100 behaviors and a specific set of open-weight target models/defenses (Llama-3-8B, Qwen2.5 7B/32B, CB, LAT, CAT, PolyGuard) -- no results are reported against closed-source commercial APIs or against defenses not covered by these adapters.

datasets_used: JailbreakBench (100 malicious behaviors; split 60 training / 40 held-out, stratified by behavior category)
quantitative_results: EVUS on Circuit Breaker (train behaviors): IHO 0.82 vs. best baseline (INPAINTING) 0.63
EVUS on Circuit Breaker + PolyGuard detector (train behaviors): IHO 0.77 vs. all baselines below 0.55
EVUS on LAT (train behaviors): IHO 0.78 vs. next-best 0.63
EVUS on CAT (train behaviors): IHO 0.79 vs. next-best 0.70
EVUS on held-out behaviors, Circuit Breaker: IHO 0.82; Circuit Breaker+detector: IHO 0.79
Cross-model transfer: baselines drop to 0.44-0.64 EVUS on the most robust targets while transferred IHO policies mostly exceed 0.70
Amortized FLOPs per training behavior on Qwen-2.5-7B: DPO training cycle 7.49e17, cycle sampling 2.21e17, DPO sampling 3.78e16
~76% reduction in per-sample inference FLOPs from reducing INPAINTING's 64-token/75-step config to IHO's 32-token/32-step config
IHO reaches near-ceiling cumulative EASR(*,0.8) within ~100 sampled generations, ahead of JR1+, GCG+, PAIR+, INPAINTING at the same sample count
Attack budget for all baselines except PAIR(default): 1024 target-model generations per behavior
baselines_compared: PAIR / PAIR+ (LLM-based iterative jailbreak attacker, default and budget-matched)
INPAINTING (untrained diffusion-inpainting attacker from Lüdke et al., the direct predecessor IHO builds on)
